title: Microlocal category for a closed symplectic manifold
speaker: Dmitry Tamarkin

proof_stages.label: Program and cotangent reduction
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames a work-in-progress construction of a DG category for a closed symplectic manifold, analogous to the Fukaya category, by first developing a microlocal theory on cotangent bundles that can see arbitrary nonconic closed subsets of T*X.

proof_stages.label: Conification in an added cotangent direction
proof_stages.summary: A nonconic closed set L in T*X is encoded as a conic subset of T*(X x R) lying in the region k > 0 and invariant in the new base variable, so that ordinary conic microsupports can model nonconic symplectic data.

proof_stages.label: Positive microlocal quotient
proof_stages.summary: The category D_{>0}(X x R) is defined as sheaves modulo those microsupported in k <= 0, making the singular support of an object well defined as a closed conic subset of the upper half-space.

proof_stages.label: Semiorthogonal projector model for D_{>0}
proof_stages.summary: A semiorthogonal decomposition is produced by convolution with the half-space kernel below the diagonal, giving explicit positive representatives and canonical maps comparing an object with its positive R-translates.

proof_stages.label: Legendrian for linear symplectic families
proof_stages.summary: Motivated by covering a closed symplectic manifold by balls, the lecture first treats the linear model: a family of linear symplectomorphisms of T*R^n is converted from its action graph, using primitives of the contact form, into a Legendrian whose conification is the intended sheaf microsupport.

proof_stages.label: Local-to-global quantization on the universal cover of Sp(2n)
proof_stages.summary: After passing to the universal cover of the symplectic group, local calculations on small disks show that the category of sheaves microsupported on the conified Legendrian is A-mod, and simple-connectedness plus H^2-vanishing globalize this to local systems and yield a distinguished quantization object Q.

proof_stages.label: Maslov, GL_n, and monoidal DG structure of Q
proof_stages.summary: The quantization object Q has the expected identity fiber, central elements of the universal cover act by the Maslov shift, its restriction along GL_n differs from the obvious graph kernel by a sign local system, and its derived convolution algebra lifts uniquely to a DG/A-infinity monoidal structure.

proof_stages.label: Nonlinear ball-embedding quantization by deformation to the linearization
proof_stages.summary: For families of symplectic embeddings of a ball into T*R^n, a Legendrian and conic open quotient are constructed, projectors localize to the ball, and—when the differential lifts to the universal cover of Sp(2n)—the resulting microsupport category is local systems on the parameter space, proved by dilating the embedding to its linearization and invoking the linear theory.

examples.name: Epigraph sheaf of a function
examples.note: For a function f on X, the constant sheaf or a local system on the closed epigraph {t >= f(x)} is offered as a typical representative in D_{>0}, making the positive-direction intuition concrete rather than producing a new idea.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs the epigraph sheaf for a function as the constant sheaf on {t≥f(x)} and checks that it lies in the positive category via stability under convolution

passes.reason: An epigraph constant sheaf for an arbitrary function is presented as a typical object of the positive microlocal category, not as a failure case.

examples.name: One-dimensional 90-degree rotation computation
examples.note: In T*R, quantizing a quarter-turn and composing it shows a reflection shifted by one and a full turn shifted by two, illustrating the central Maslov shift of the quantization object Q.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes to R^1, quantizes a 90-degree rotation, and traces its iterated compositions to get a reflection shifted by one and then a two-unit shift after four rotations

passes.reason: The quarter-turn in the one-dimensional symplectic plane is a regular orthogonal symplectic map used to illustrate the Maslov shift computation, not to refute a claim.

The 90-degree rotation is computed in the one-dimensional cotangent case, the minimal nontrivial base case for the symplectic linear story, and is used illustratively to see the shift/Maslov behavior.
The 90-degree rotation is used in the minimal one-dimensional symplectic case as a base computation illustrating the Maslov shift.
the quarter-turn in the one-dimensional cotangent plane is used as a simple illustrative computation of the Maslov shift, not as an extremal or obstructing case

The one-dimensional quarter-turn is a minimal regular test computation used to illustrate the Maslov shift, not as a failure case.

examples.name: Symplectic capacities from localization projectors
examples.note: The speaker mentions that projectors for conic open subsets can be used to define symplectic capacities, illustrating that these localization kernels carry geometric information.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only says that localization projectors can be used to define symplectic capacities, without carrying out the definition or any computation

passes.reason: Localization projectors yielding symplectic capacities are cited as a systematic source of invariants rather than as sporadic, limiting, optimal objects or counterexamples.
Symplectic capacities obtained from localization projectors are cited as ordinary invariants carried by the projectors, not as special structural exceptions or counterexamples.
A symplectic capacity is an invariant measuring an optimal size/embedding threshold, here cited as information encoded by localization projectors.
